article.title: Best Dog-Friendly Holidays in the UK 2026: Cottages, Campsites & Hotels
article.body_html:
Best Dog-Friendly Holidays in the UK 2026: Cottages, Campsites & Hotels

Target: mymuttlife.co.uk | Word count: ~3,200 | SEO keyword: "dog friendly holidays UK 2026"
Affiliate opportunities: Sykes Cottages, Canine Cottages, Hoseasons, Amazon (travel gear)

Taking your dog on holiday shouldn't feel like an obstacle course of "no pets" policies and hidden surcharges. The good news? The UK has never been more dog-friendly. Over 225,000 dogs went on a Sykes Holiday Cottages break in 2025 alone, and dog bookings already make up 31% of all 2026 reservations.

Whether you fancy a windswept cottage on the Northumberland coast, a glamping pod in the Lake District, or a country hotel with a doggy turndown service, this guide covers everything you need to plan the perfect holiday with your four-legged friend.

The 10 Best Dog-Friendly Holiday Destinations in the UK (2026)

Based on booking data from the UK's biggest holiday cottage providers, these are the destinations dog owners are flocking to this year.

1. The Lake District, Cumbria

Why dogs love it: Over 3,000 dog-friendly bookings already confirmed for 2026. Sixteen lakes, countless fell walks, and some of the most beautiful scenery in England. William Wordsworth was inspired here — your Labrador will be too.

Best for: Active dogs who love swimming and long walks
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Grizedale Forest — miles of off-lead trails through ancient woodland

  • Tarn Hows — gentle circular walk with stunning views (dogs on leads near livestock)

  • Numerous dog-friendly pubs, including The Drunken Duck and The Old Dungeon Ghyll

Where to stay:


  • Holiday cottages from ~£400/week (off-peak) to £1,200+ (peak summer)

  • Dog-friendly glamping pods from £80/night

  • [Affiliate: Sykes Cottages Lake District dog-friendly properties]

Top tip: Visit April–June or September–October for fewer crowds. Many fell walks have lambing restrictions from March to May — check before you go.

2. Northumberland

Why dogs love it: Nearly 2,000 pet-friendly stays booked for 2026. Over 60 miles of dramatic coastline, most of it completely dog-friendly year-round (no seasonal beach bans). Plus Kielder Water & Forest Park — England's largest forest.

Best for: Dogs who love empty beaches and wide-open spaces
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Bamburgh Beach — regularly voted the UK's best beach, dogs welcome all year

  • Hadrian's Wall Path — 84-mile trail with dogs welcome

  • Holy Island of Lindisfarne — spectacular tidal island (check tide times!)

  • St Oswald's Way — 97-mile coastal and countryside path

Where to stay:


  • Coastal cottages from ~£350/week

  • Farm stays from ~£300/week

  • [Affiliate: Canine Cottages Northumberland collection]

Top tip: Northumberland is brilliantly uncrowded compared to Cornwall. If your dog is reactive or nervous around other dogs, this is ideal — you can walk for miles without seeing another soul.

3. Scottish Highlands & Islands

Why dogs love it: Vast, untamed landscapes where dogs can run free. Scotland's access laws (Right to Roam) mean you can walk almost anywhere, though dogs should be kept under control near livestock and ground-nesting birds.

Best for: Adventure dogs, confident off-lead dogs, owners who want remote escapes
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Glen Affric — "the most beautiful glen in Scotland"

  • Loch Lomond — swimming, walking, boat trips (many allow dogs)

  • The NC500 — Scotland's answer to Route 66, with dog-friendly stops throughout

  • Scottish islands like Skye, Mull, and Lewis — largely dog-paradise

Where to stay:


  • Remote cottages from ~£400/week

  • Bothies (basic mountain shelters) — free!

  • Dog-friendly B&Bs from ~£70/night

  • [Affiliate: Hoseasons Scottish Highlands dog-friendly lodges]

Top tip: Midges can be brutal from late May to September, especially near standing water. Bring a dog-safe insect repellent and avoid dawn/dusk walks near lochs during midge season.

4. Cornwall

Why dogs love it: The UK's classic seaside holiday destination, with over 300 beaches and a thriving dog-friendly culture. Many cafés and restaurants welcome dogs, and surf schools even offer doggy surfing lessons.

Best for: Beach-loving dogs, families, first-time dog holidaymakers
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Perranporth Beach — 3 miles of golden sand, dogs welcome year-round on most sections

  • The South West Coast Path — 630 miles of stunning coastal trail

  • Bodmin Moor — wild moorland walking with few restrictions

  • Dog-friendly fish and chip shops in virtually every village

Where to stay:


  • Holiday cottages from ~£500/week (off-peak) to £2,000+ (August)

  • Dog-friendly holiday parks from £300/week

  • [Affiliate: Dog Friendly Cottages Cornwall collection]

Top tip: Many Cornish beaches have seasonal dog bans from May to September (typically 10am–6pm). Always check the specific beach rules before visiting. Head to north Cornwall for more year-round access.

5. North Wales

Why dogs love it: Snowdonia's mountains, Anglesey's beaches, and the Welsh countryside all within a compact area. Wales is genuinely one of the most dog-friendly nations in the UK — most pubs, cafés, and attractions welcome dogs.

Best for: Active families, mountain-loving dogs
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Snowdon (Yr Wyddfa) — dogs allowed on all routes (on lead near summit in busy periods)

  • Llanddwyn Beach, Anglesey — stunning and quiet

  • Portmeirion — the famous Italianate village allows dogs in the grounds

  • Zip World Fforest — you can't zip-line with your dog, but the forest trails are excellent

Where to stay:


  • Farmhouse cottages from ~£350/week

  • Coastal apartments from ~£400/week

  • Camping and glamping from ~£25/night

  • [Affiliate: Sykes Cottages North Wales dog-friendly]

Top tip: Welsh weather is unpredictable. Pack layers and waterproofs for both you and your dog, even in summer.

6. Dorset

Why dogs love it: The Jurassic Coast, rolling countryside, and Thomas Hardy's literary landscapes. Dorset is gentle, accessible, and perfect for older dogs or those who prefer shorter walks.

Best for: Older dogs, calmer breeds, couples
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Studland Bay — one of the best dog-friendly beaches in southern England

  • Lulworth Cove — dramatic coastline walking

  • Cranborne Chase AONB — peaceful countryside rambles

  • The many dog-friendly pubs of Dorchester and Weymouth

Where to stay:


  • Coastal cottages from ~£450/week

  • Country hotels from ~£100/night (check pet policies)

  • [Affiliate: Canine Cottages Dorset]

7. Yorkshire Dales

Why dogs love it: Classic English countryside at its finest. Stone walls, green valleys, tumbling waterfalls, and some of the friendliest village pubs in the country.

Best for: Walking-obsessed dogs, all breeds
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Aysgarth Falls — beautiful waterfall walk

  • Malham Cove — dramatic limestone amphitheatre

  • The Dales Way — 79-mile trail from Ilkley to the Lake District

  • Bolton Abbey — gorgeous riverside walks (dogs on leads in parts)

Where to stay:


  • Stone cottages from ~£350/week

  • Dog-friendly pubs with rooms from ~£90/night

  • [Affiliate: Sykes Cottages Yorkshire Dales]

8. North York Moors & Coast

Why dogs love it: Heather-covered moorland meets dramatic coastline. Whitby, Robin Hood's Bay, and Scarborough all have strong dog-friendly cultures, and the North York Moors are vast and uncrowded.

Best for: Dogs who love variety — moors one day, beach the next
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Robin Hood's Bay — charming fishing village with year-round dog beach

  • The Cleveland Way — 109-mile trail through moors and coast

  • Whitby — fish and chips on the harbour with your dog

  • Dalby Forest — excellent forest trails

9. Southern Scotland (Borders & Dumfries)

Why dogs love it: Beautifully overlooked. Rolling hills, historic castles, and far fewer tourists than the Highlands. Over 600 dog-friendly bookings for 2026 shows it's a rising star.

Best for: Dogs who prefer quiet, owners who want value for money
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • St Abb's Head — spectacular clifftop walks and nature reserve

  • Galloway Forest Dark Sky Park — amazing stargazing (dogs don't care, but you will)

  • The River Tweed — gentle riverside walks through beautiful countryside

10. Norfolk

Why dogs love it: Flat, open landscapes perfect for dogs of all fitness levels. The Norfolk Broads offer a unique boating experience (many hire boats are dog-friendly), and the north Norfolk coast has some of the best dog beaches in England.

Best for: Older dogs, less mobile owners, families with young children
Dog-friendly highlights:


  • Holkham Beach — vast, flat, and stunning

  • Wells-next-the-Sea — dog-friendly beach huts and pine woods

  • The Norfolk Broads — dog-friendly boat hire from ~£150/day

  • Cromer and Sheringham — classic seaside towns

Types of Dog-Friendly Accommodation

Holiday Cottages (Best All-Rounder)

Price range: £300–£2,000+/week depending on location and season Pros: Private space, often with enclosed gardens, full kitchen, home comforts Cons: Some charge per-dog supplements (£20–£50/stay), may have breed/size restrictions Best providers:
  • Sykes Holiday Cottages — 13,000+ dog-friendly properties
  • Canine Cottages — specialist, all properties verified dog-friendly
  • Dog Friendly Cottages — good search filters
  • National Trust — nearly 300 dog-friendly cottages in stunning locations

Camping & Glamping (Budget-Friendly)

Price range: £15–£120/night Pros: Most affordable, dogs love the outdoors, great for sociable dogs Cons: Weather-dependent, fewer amenities, some sites restrict dog numbers Best for: Spring and summer holidays Look for: Sites with enclosed pitches if your dog isn't great off-lead

Hotels (Easiest)

Price range: £80–£250+/night Pros: No cooking, housekeeping, sometimes offer doggy extras (beds, treats, bowls) Cons: Can feel restrictive, often charged per dog per night (£10–£30), limited exercise space Dog-friendly hotel chains:
  • Premier Inn — dogs allowed in selected rooms (£0 supplement at most locations)
  • Warner Leisure Hotels — adults-only with dog-friendly options
  • Marriott Bonvoy — many UK properties accept dogs

Holiday Parks (Families)

Price range: £300–£1,500/week Pros: Entertainment for kids, facilities on-site, often have dog-walking areas Cons: Can be busy, some restrict dog breeds Best providers:
  • Hoseasons — dogs go free on selected spring breaks
  • Haven — dog-friendly caravans available
  • Center Parcs — dogs allowed in selected lodges (sell out fast!)

Essential Packing List for Dog Holidays

Don't leave home without these:

The Basics

  • [ ] Dog food (enough for the trip plus 2 extra days — you might not find their brand locally)
  • [ ] Bowls (collapsible ones save space)
  • [ ] Lead and harness (plus a spare — leads break at the worst times)
  • [ ] Poo bags (more than you think you need)
  • [ ] Dog bed or familiar blanket (reduces anxiety in new places)
  • [ ] Towels (at least 2 — for muddy paws and post-swim drying)

Health & Safety

  • [ ] Vaccination records (some accommodation requires proof)
  • [ ] Dog first-aid kit (tick remover, antiseptic wipes, bandages)
  • [ ] Tick/flea treatment (especially in rural areas)
  • [ ] Any medications
  • [ ] Microchip details (just in case)
  • [ ] Local vet details (research before you go)

Nice to Have

  • [ ] Dog drying coat — a game-changer in the rain
  • [ ] Long line (for training or areas requiring leads)
  • [ ] Favourite toy (for settling in unfamiliar places)
  • [ ] Car seat cover or crate (for travel)
  • [ ] Dog-safe sun cream (for pale-nosed breeds in summer)
  • [ ] LED collar or light (for evening walks in the countryside)

Tips for a Stress-Free Dog Holiday

Before You Book

  1. Check the pet policy thoroughly — some "dog-friendly" places mean "one small dog only." Look for max number, size limits, and breed restrictions.
  2. Ask about enclosed gardens — essential if your dog isn't reliable off-lead. Sites like Canine Cottages rate properties for garden security.
  3. Check for extra charges — per-dog supplements can add up, especially with multiple dogs.
  4. Read recent reviews — other dog owners will flag any issues.

During Your Stay

  1. Stick to routines — feed and walk at normal times to reduce anxiety.
  2. Don't leave your dog alone — many properties prohibit it, and your dog may be anxious in a new place. If you need a meal out, find a dog-friendly restaurant.
  3. Carry water — not all walks have natural water sources, and saltwater doesn't count.
  4. Check for livestock — put your dog on a lead anywhere near sheep, cattle, or horses. This is a legal requirement in many areas and genuinely important for safety.

Coming Home

  1. Check for ticks — especially after walks through long grass, bracken, or woodland.
  2. Wash everything — sandy towels, muddy coats, and slobbery car seats.
  3. Watch for post-holiday stress — some dogs feel unsettled after a change of routine. Extra walks and attention help.

Budget Guide: What to Expect to Spend

TypeOff-Peak (per week)Peak Summer (per week)Dog Supplement
Budget cottage£300–£500£600–£1,000£0–£30/stay
Mid-range cottage£500–£900£1,000–£1,500£20–£50/stay
Luxury cottage£900–£2,000+£1,500–£3,000+Often included
Camping pitch£15–£30/night£25–£50/night£0–£5/night
Glamping pod£60–£120/night£100–£200/night£10–£20/stay
Hotel room£80–£150/night£120–£250+/night£10–£30/night
Money-saving tip: Book early for summer 2026 — dog-friendly properties sell out fast, especially in Cornwall and the Lake District. Off-peak (October–March, excluding half-terms) offers the best value, and many dogs actually prefer cooler weather for long walks.

Final Thoughts

A holiday with your dog isn't a compromise — it's often better than going without them. There's nothing quite like exploring a new beach together, collapsing in front of a log fire after a long walk, or watching your dog discover the sea for the first time.

The UK is genuinely brilliant for dog holidays. From the untouched beaches of Northumberland to the mountains of Snowdonia, there's a perfect trip waiting for every dog and every budget. The hardest part? Choosing where to go first.

Start planning early, check the pet policy twice, and pack more towels than you think you need. Your dog will thank you for it.

Looking for more dog-friendly inspiration? Check out our Dog-Friendly Beaches UK Guide and Best Dog Walking Essentials.

Affiliate Links to Insert:

SEO Notes:


  • Primary keyword: "dog friendly holidays UK 2026"

  • Secondary: "best dog friendly cottages UK", "dog friendly campsites UK", "holidays with dogs UK"

  • Schema: FAQ (use Tips section), Article, HowTo (packing list)

  • Internal links: dog-friendly beaches guide, walking essentials guide
